item.title: 数以百万计的关节疼痛和骨关节炎患者缺少最强大的治疗方法--ScienceDaily
item.description:
Stiff knees, sore hips, and persistent joint pain are often brushed off as normal signs of aging. But osteoarthritis, the most common joint disease worldwide, is not being treated in line with what research actually shows. Experts say the biggest gap is not a lack of options, but a failure to use the most effective one.

The most powerful treatment is not found in a prescription bottle or an operating room. It is movement. Yet in many countries and healthcare systems, patients are rarely directed toward the one therapy proven to reduce pain and protect joints: exercise.

Exercise is widely recognized as one of the most effective treatments for long term joint conditions such as osteoarthritis. Even so, only a minority of patients are prescribed it.

Studies from Ireland, the UK, Norway, and the United States reveal a consistent pattern. Fewer than half of people diagnosed with osteoarthritis are referred to exercise programs or physical therapy by their primary care provider. More than 60 percent receive treatments that clinical guidelines do not recommend, and about 40 percent are referred to a surgeon before non surgical options have been properly explored.

Why Exercise Protects Your Joints

To understand why this matters, it helps to look at how joints actually function. Osteoarthritis is the most common type of arthritis, already affecting more than 595 million people globally.

A major analysis published in The Lancet estimates that the number of people living with osteoarthritis could approach one billion by 2050. Longer lifespans, more sedentary habits, and rising rates of overweight and obesity are fueling this increase.

Regular physical activity does more than ease symptoms. It can biologically and physically lower the risk of developing osteoarthritis and reduce its severity.

Cartilage, the smooth tissue that cushions the ends of bones, does not have its own blood supply. It depends on movement to stay healthy.

When you walk or put weight on a joint, cartilage is gently compressed, pushing fluid out. When the pressure is released, it draws fluid back in, bringing nutrients and natural lubricants with it. Each step helps nourish and maintain the joint.

This is why describing osteoarthritis as simple "wear and tear" is misleading. Joints are not like tires that inevitably break down over time.

Instead, osteoarthritis is better understood as a long process of breakdown and repair. Regular movement plays a central role in supporting healing and maintaining the health of the entire joint.

Osteoarthritis Is a Whole Joint Disease

Osteoarthritis does not affect cartilage alone. It involves the entire joint, including joint fluid, underlying bone, ligaments, surrounding muscles, and even the nerves that control movement.

Targeted exercise addresses many of these components at once. Muscle weakness is one of the earliest warning signs of osteoarthritis, and resistance training can help reverse it. Strong evidence shows that weak muscles increase the risk of both developing osteoarthritis and experiencing faster progression.

Neuromuscular exercise programs such as GLA:D® (Good Life with osteoArthritis: Denmark) are designed specifically for people with hip and knee osteoarthritis. Typically offered in supervised group sessions led by physical therapists, these programs focus on improving movement quality, balance, and strength. The goal is to enhance joint stability and rebuild confidence in movement.

Participants in these programs have reported meaningful reductions in pain, better joint function, and improved quality of life lasting up to 12 months after finishing the program.

Exercise benefits the entire body. It has proven advantages across more than 26 chronic diseases. In osteoarthritis, its impact goes beyond strengthening muscles and supporting cartilage. It also addresses inflammation, metabolic changes, and hormonal shifts that contribute to the disease.

Inflammation, Obesity, and Joint Damage

Obesity is a major risk factor for osteoarthritis, and the reason is not just added stress on the joints. Excess body fat is linked to higher levels of inflammatory molecules in the bloodstream and joint tissues. These substances can damage cartilage and speed up disease progression.

Regular physical activity helps counter these effects at a molecular level. It can lower inflammatory markers, reduce cell damage, and even influence gene expression in ways that support joint health.

Exercise Before Surgery

There are currently no medications that can change the underlying course of osteoarthritis. Joint replacement surgery can be transformative for some people, but it is a major procedure and does not work equally well for everyone.

Exercise should be the first step and remain part of care at every stage of the disease. It carries far fewer risks than surgery and provides broad health benefits beyond the joints.

Osteoarthritis is not simply about "worn out" joints. It is shaped by muscle strength, inflammation, metabolism, and lifestyle factors.

Regular, structured exercise addresses many of these drivers at the same time, helping protect cartilage, support the entire joint, and improve overall health. Before turning to surgery, movement remains one of the most effective treatments available.
item.author: ScienceDaily

item.title: 这位母亲患有一个婴儿头部大小的宫颈癌肿瘤。一项临床试验挽救了她的生命。-今日美国
item.description:
Updated March 4, 2026, 4:35 p.m. ET

Aricca Wallace never thought she'd still be here. In fact, in 2012, she was told she likely had less than a year to live.

The Kansas mother of two, now 49, knew something was wrong when she was 33 and noticed bleeding during intercourse. Her gynecologist at the time told her it was likely an issue with her intrauterine device.

Two years later, she learned it wasn't.

Instead, the bleeding was caused by hemorrhaging from a tumor − one roughly the size of a baby's head. Her annual pap smears had missed it. She had Stage 3 cervical cancer.

"It came as a complete shock," Wallace says.

Wallace's prognosis wasn't good. She underwent eighteen rounds of chemotherapy and 31 rounds of radiation in total, she says. But in January 2012, scans revealed her cancer had spread. A month after that, her doctors confirmed the worst: Curing the cancer was no longer possible. In all likelihood, she had less than a year to live.

"That was hard to take. But as a mom and a wife, I said, 'I have too much more to live for. I don't accept that,' " Wallace says. "And so, I started just taking the chemo to control it and buy us some time."

In May of 2012, Wallace received news that changed everything. A clinical trial in Maryland needed candidates to test a new cervical cancer treatment. The study was conducted through a collaboration between the National Cancer Institute and the Rutgers Cancer Institute, together with RWJBarnabas Health.

Wallace was a perfect fit for it − and became one of the trial's first participants.

At that time, no one knew how the treatment would pan out.

Wallace has been cancer-free for over 13 years.

Now, she is raising awareness about cervical cancer and where the fight against the disease stands today. And she's doing so alongside the doctor who treated her during the trial, Dr. Christian Hinrichs, co-director of the Duncan and Nancy MacMillan Cancer Immunology and Metabolism Center of Excellence at Rutgers Cancer Institute.

"You have to be mentally strong. You have to be faithfully strong," Wallace says. "My mindset was, it's not up to a doctor of when my time here's done. It's not. I mean, they can tell me scientifically that they don't have anything that can help me and that I'm going to be gone in a year. But ultimately, that's not their call."

'We were running out of options pretty quickly'

Cervical cancer is notoriously difficult to treat, Hinrichs says.

Most commonly caused by human papillomavirus, or HPV, the disease has a five-year relative survival rate of 67% for all those diagnosed, per the National Cancer Institute. For those diagnosed after the disease has spread to nearby tissues, organs or regional lymph nodes, that rate is 60%. For those diagnosed after it's spread to a distant part of the body, it's 19%.

When Wallace arrived in Maryland, "we were running out of options pretty quickly," she says. She became one of the lucky ones.

During Wallace's trial, Hinrichs removed one of her tumors through surgery and then grew immune cells, or T cells, trained to fight the tumor up to large numbers in a laboratory. He sent the most effective of those back into Wallace's body to fight the rest of her cancer.

The treatment has drawbacks, Hinrichs says. It requires surgery as well as time to generate the cancer-fighting cells. It's also not approved as standard therapy and continues to undergo research. Today, Hinrichs says, his clinical trials are focused on using genetically engineered cells from patients' blood to fight the cancer, sidestepping surgery.

Hinrichs was immediately struck by Wallace's positivity, something essential in his line of work.

"I'm 100% trying to wipe out that cancer, and I really need that other person who's part of this effort to be with me," Hinrichs says. "And I say that because it can be tough. The treatments can be tough. The other life events that happen while you're trying to battle the cancer, they can be tough too. And me, I'm committed. And I feel like I kind of need you to be committed too."

Wallace recalls days during the trial when she felt too weak to get out of bed. Unlike with standard chemotherapy, she says, with this new treatment, doctors couldn't say for certain when she'd start to feel better again.

But even if the trial didn't turn out in her favor, Wallace says it would still have been worth it, if only for her loved ones to know she tried everything.

"I needed to make sure that, if this was going to take me, I had proven to my kids and family that I had thrown the kitchen sink at it, and I'd given everything that I could," she says. "If it worked, then amazing, we did it. And if it didn't? Then they knew I went down with a big fight."

'You're saving grandmas'

The big fight paid off. In September 2012, over 50% of Wallace's tumors were gone, she says. By December, she was cancer-free.

When Wallace was first diagnosed, her children were 8 and 12. Now, they're 23 and 27. She lives about two hours from Kansas City and enjoys being a grandmother to her 2-year-old and 1-month-old grandsons.

"Holding my grandson that's 2 for the first time two years ago was pretty surreal for me and for my husband, because we just didn't think we'd be here," Wallace says. "But we are. And just enjoying life and just continuing to tell people to advocate for themselves and trying to continue to be an inspiration to others."

When she hit 10 years cancer-free, Wallace celebrated with a "cancerversary" party. Hinrichs and his kids attended. He and Wallace remain close friends today.

Wallace tears up recalling the day one of her sons, then 10, looked up at her doctors and said, "Thank you for saving my mom." To her, that alone sums up the importance of what researchers like Hinrichs are working on.

"I told him, I'm like, 'That's what you guys are doing,' " she says. " 'You researchers, you're saving moms. You're saving wives. You're saving grandmas.' "

item.title: 每天吃鸡蛋对身体有什么影响- marthastewart.com
item.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Eating eggs daily can support your health by providing essential nutrients, boosting immunity, and promoting fullness.
  • Eggs are rich in high-quality protein, vitamins, and minerals that help repair tissues, build muscles, and support eye health.
  • Cooking methods matter—boiling or poaching eggs keeps them healthy, while frying adds extra calories and fat.

No breakfast spread is complete without eggs. They're versatile, tasty, and easily prepared in minutes. You might even find yourself eating eggs daily, especially if you enjoy boiling eggs in bulk or baking quiche. But is it healthy to consume eggs so often? And what will happen if you do? We chatted with dietitians to learn what happens when you eat eggs every day, along with tips to do so healthfully.

  • Laurie Allen, MEd, RDN, LDN, registered dietitian, associate professor, and didactic program director at University of North Carolina Greensboro
  • Michelle Newman, MS, RDN, CDCES, registered nutritionist, diabetes care and education specialist, dietitian coordinator at The Molly Diabetes Education and Management Center for Adults and Children at Hackensack University Medical Center

What Happens When You Eat Eggs

Dietitians explain how eating eggs affects your body.

Increases Protein Intake

"Eggs are an excellent source of high-quality protein," says Laurie Allen, MEd, RDN, LDN, associate professor and didactic program director at the University of North Carolina Greensboro. This means they contain all the essential amino acids the body needs but can't make on its own. "These amino acids are crucial because they build and repair tissue, support muscle growth, and help the body carry out various bodily functions," says Michelle Newman, MS, RDN, CDCES, registered nutritionist, diabetes care and education specialist, dietitian coordinator at The Molly Diabetes Education and Management Center for Adults and Children at Hackensack University Medical Center. "One large egg provides approximately 6 grams of protein, making eggs an efficient and accessible way to meet daily protein needs," Allen notes.

Supports Immune Function

Regularly eating eggs can help support immune function. That's because your immune system depends on dietary protein (which is found in eggs) to produce immune proteins like antibodies, says Allen. These proteins "allow the body to respond quickly to threats like bacteria and viruses," she notes. Eggs also contain selenium, vitamin A, vitamin B12, and zinc, all of which support healthy immune function, according to Newman.

What's more, "egg yolks are one of the few natural food sources of vitamin D," says Newman. Vitamin D helps regulate both your innate immunity (the body's first line of defense) and adaptive immunity (the body's specialized, targeted defense), Newman says.

Enhances Satiety

When part of a balanced diet, eggs can help boost satiety and fullness. This is due to their protein and fat content, according to Allen. Together, these nutrients slow gastric emptying (i.e., the rate at which food moves through the digestive system), which extends the feeling of fullness after a meal, Allen says. "Protein and fat also stimulate the release of appetite-regulating hormones, including peptide YY and GLP-1, which signal to the brain that the body is satisfied," says Allen. So, eggs can naturally reduce overall calorie consumption and snacking between meals, Newman says.

Helps Eye Health

When you eat eggs every day, you'll increase your intake of eye-friendly nutrients. "Eggs support eye health because the yolk contains lutein and zeaxanthin," says Allen. These compounds accumulate in the part of the eye responsible for central vision. Here, "they help filter blue light and protect eye tissue from damage caused by light exposure and normal aging," says Allen. Not to mention, a higher intake of lutein and zeaxanthin has been associated with a lower risk of age-related macular degeneration, says Allen.

How Many Eggs to Eat

"For most generally healthy adults, one to two eggs per day is considered safe," says Allen. But as with many foods, there are some exceptions.

Some people have familial hypercholesterolemia, a condition that genetically predisposes them to elevated cholesterol levels. This increases your sensitivity to dietary cholesterol, which is found in eggs. In this case, it's often recommended to limit egg consumption to three to four per week, says Allen.

Also, eggs are among the most common allergens, according to the USDA. If you're allergic to eggs, avoid them completely.

"Consuming raw or undercooked eggs, such as those with runny yolks, carries a risk of Salmonella infection," says Newman. For safety, cook eggs thoroughly, especially when serving vulnerable people like young children, older adults, pregnant people, and individuals with weakened immune systems, she says.

Cooking Methods Matter

While eggs are nutritionally impressive, it's important to consider your cooking method—especially if you're eating them daily. The method will affect the egg's final nutritional profile and its effects on the body.

For example, if you'd like to maximize their nutritional benefits without adding extra calories or fat, poaching or boiling eggs is the way to go. "These methods don't require oil or butter, so it keeps the calorie count lower compared to other preparations," Newman says. Meanwhile, frying eggs in butter (or scrambling them with cream) will increase the meal's saturated fat and total calorie content, says Allen. This is something to keep in mind if you'd like to limit either component.

Alternatively, eggs can serve as a vehicle for nutritious ingredients. "Try whisking a quarter cup of cottage cheese and a handful of vegetables into scrambled eggs," says Allen. It's a simple way to increase the meal's protein and micronutrients without significantly changing the cooking process, she says.
item.author: marthastewart.com
